author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Does the Soul Take the Body’s Clothes Also Away with It?
title: Does the Soul Take the Body’s Clothes Also Away with It?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Chapter 29 [XIX.]—Does the Soul Take the Body’s Clothes Also Away with It?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: If, indeed, the soul were body, and the form were also a corporeal figure in which it sees itself in dreams, on the ground that it received its expression from the body in which it is enclosed: not a human being, if he lost a limb, would in dreams see himself bereft of the amputated member, although actually deprived of it.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: On the contrary, he would always appear to himself entire and unmutilated, from the circumstance that no part has been cut away from the soul itself.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: But since persons sometimes see themselves whole and sometimes mutilated in limb, when this happens to be their actual plight, what else does this fact show than that the soul, both in respect of other things seen by it in dreams and in reference to the body, bears about, hither and thither, not their reality, but only their resemblance?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: The soul’s joy, however, or sadness, its pleasure or pain, are severally real emotions, whether experienced in actual or in apparent bodies.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Have you not yourself said (and with perfect truth): “Aliments and vestments are not wanted by the soul, but only by the body”?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Why, then, did the rich man in hell crave for the drop of water?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Why did holy Samuel appear after his death (as you have yourself noticed) clothed in his usual garments?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Did the one wish to repair the ruins of the soul, as of the flesh, by the aliment of water?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Did the other quit life with his clothes on him?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now in the former case there was a real suffering, which tormented the soul; but not a real body, such as required food.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: While the latter might have seemed to be clothed, not as being a veritable body, but a soul only, having the semblance of a body with a dress.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For although the soul extends and contracts itself to suit the members of the body, it does not similarly adapt itself to the clothes, so as to fit its form to them.
